items.title: Researchers 'hopeful' after North Atlantic right whale baby boom
items.summary_auto: Twenty-three North Atlantic right whales were born during this year's calving season — the highest number in more than 15 years.

items.title: Liberals face questions after former IRGC official was reportedly granted visa for FIFA event
Podcasters accused of ‘harassment’ campaign against former AHS leaders want search order tossed
items.summary_auto: A lawyer for two podcasters asked a judge Wednesday to set aside a contempt citation and an “extraordinary” court order that was used to search their homes last year.

items.title: Indigenous Tourism Association of Canada 'cash flow' issues hurting small businesses owed money
items.summary_auto: Carey Perkins is among several small business owners counting on funding through the Indigenous Tourism Association of Canada they have not received.

items.title: RCMP identify Australian woman missing in Cape Breton Highlands National Park
items.summary_auto: Denise Ann Williams, 62, was last heard from April 15 when she indicated she was travelling to Chéticamp, N.S.

items.title: Separatist group ordered to pull down Alberta electoral list
Liberals use majority to move some parliamentary committees behind closed doors
items.summary_auto: It’s been less than a week since the federal Liberals took control of parliamentary committees, and they’re using their new-found power to take proceedings behind closed doors.

items.title: Myanmar’s Aung San Suu Kyi to serve rest of prison term under house arrest
items.summary_auto: Myanmar’s detained former leader Aung San Suu Kyi has been moved from prison to house arrest, state television announced Thursday night.

items.title: Real GDP rose 0.2 per cent in February says StatsCan, marking four straight months of growth
items.summary_auto: GDP growth was led by a 1.8 per cent gain in the manufacturing sector, though manufacturing activity was still down compared to a year ago following nearly a year of tariff threats from the U.S.

items.title: Quebec mosque shooter wants to move to medium-security prison due to safety concerns
items.summary_auto: Alexandre Bissonnette, who pleaded guilty to killing six men at a Quebec City mosque in 2017, is requesting to be moved to a medium-security prison, citing safety concerns. The petition outlines that his current condition at Port-Cartier is a violation of his Charter rights.

items.title: Occupied West Bank violence leaves students ‘afraid to attend school’
items.summary_auto: Palestinians in the occupied West Bank say they fear for their children’s safety after an attack at a school killed two people, including a 14-year-old — violence they blame on Israeli settlers.

items.title: Maker of Dove soap, Axe deodorant warns of small, 'frequent' price hikes due to war in Iran
items.summary_auto: Unilever said on Thursday it would raise prices to soften the hit from higher-than-expected costs driven by the Iran war, even as it reported first-quarter underlying sales growth ahead of analysts' forecasts.

items.title: Supreme Court of Canada to hear appeal of man behind Winston Churchill portrait heist in Ottawa
items.summary_auto: Jeffrey Wood, the man who stole The Roaring Lion portrait of Winston Churchill from Ottawa's Château Laurier hotel between Christmas 2021 and early January 2022, will be able to appeal his jail sentence.

items.title: Suspect in stabbing of 2 Jewish men had been flagged by U.K. counterterrorism program
items.summary_auto: The British government on Thursday said the country was facing an antisemitism "emergency" and pledged funding to increase security for Jewish communities, but also faced heavy criticism after a string of arson attacks and a double stabbing on Wednesday.

items.title: Search site dismantled at Winnipeg-area landfill, where remains of First Nations women killed by serial killer were recovered
items.summary_auto: The search site at a Winnipeg-area landfill, where remains of Morgan Harris and Marcedes Myran were found, has officially been decommissioned.

items.title: Ontario's law to transform jails is 'collecting dust.' A new bill aims to hold the province's feet to the fire
items.summary_auto: For years, Ontario has sat on legislation meant to tackle chronic problems inside its jails — with laws passed years ago left on the books as issues of violence, overcrowding and mental health behind bars swell.

items.title: Crowd erupts at Toronto Tempo's 1st ever WNBA game as Kia Nurse makes Canadian basketball history
items.summary_auto: Well before the Toronto Tempo fell 83-78 against the Connecticut Sun, fans waited in long lines for merchandise, players went through shootaround and seats slowly filled in anticipation of tip-off.

items.title: Iran threatens 'long and painful strikes' on U.S. targets if Trump resumes bombing
items.summary_auto: Iran said on Thursday it would respond with "long and painful strikes" on U.S. positions if Washington renewed attacks, and reasserted its control over the Strait of Hormuz, complicating  U.S. plans for a coalition to reopen the waterway.

items.title: Oil prices hit new wartime high before easing, as gas prices expected to go up
items.summary_auto: The price of Brent crude oil briefly surged past $126 US a barrel early Thursday before coming back down as stalled U.S.-Iran talks raised doubts over the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and a permanent end to the Iran war.

items.title: Once a star candidate, Nenshi is losing lustre, Alberta poll suggests. Can the NDP leader recover?
items.summary_auto: Two years in, Albertans are less impressed with Nenshi than they used to be. And Albertans of almost every type are more impressed with Danielle Smith.

items.title: As free radon testing kit program ends for the spring, 2,600 Winnipeggers are still waiting
items.summary_auto: The City of Winnipeg is winding down its library program to lend free radon testing kits to patrons, but more than 2,600 people are still waiting to get the device — some after months. "It's frustrating," said Brian Itscovich, a Winnipeg resident who ordered the kit.

items.title: Budget 2026 reveals how N.L.'s economy is driven by oil, mining and fishing
items.summary_auto: Newfoundland and Labrador is once again forecasting a hefty budgetary shortfall, but is expected to lead the country by a wide margin in 2026 when it comes to GDP growth.

items.title: Germany does more hip and knee replacements than Canada. What can we learn?
items.summary_auto: Canadians are waiting months to years for a hip or knee replacement, and in some cases, paying thousands of dollars out-of-pocket to have the surgery done privately. That isn't happening in Germany.

items.title: Your past is calling: Why 'retro' landline phones are trending with adults and kids alike
items.summary_auto: It's trending with Gen Alpha as parents seek out an antidote to screen time, but the Tin Can is also just one of several landline brands surging in popularity as a growing number of consumers try to reduce their reliance on smartphones.

items.title: Are Montreal’s own policies keeping some buildings vacant — and vulnerable?
items.summary_auto: One Montreal family wonders whether a second fire at their building could have been avoided if the City of Montreal had granted them a demolition permit after the first. But the city said demolition was not an option without a replacement project. The family's experience reflects the hurdles some property owners face navigating the city’s policies around vacant buildings.

items.title: Why Mafalda, the beloved Argentine cartoon, is finally making waves in the English-speaking world
items.summary_auto: Despite knowing international success, the legendary Argentine comic strip character has only recently broken into the English-speaking world, with a translation of cartoonist Quino’s work becoming available just last year. A Montreal art café owner is hoping an official statue in the city will help spread the Mafalda love.

items.title: Montreal father faces deportation as immigration advocates decry more family separations
items.summary_auto: A young Mexican family is scrambling to convince Canadian border services to give them more time to prove he faces threats to his life in Mexico. The father's deportation date is 10 days before he's eligible to make that case.

items.title: Liberals are pitching a brand new police agency for financial crimes. How would that work?
items.summary_auto: The Liberal government is looking to set up a new federal law enforcement agency dedicated to financial crimes, a dramatic undertaking to shake up how money laundering and fraud is investigated are Canada.

items.title: Women oppose publication bans in Nunavut sexual assault case
items.summary_auto: Three women who have reported allegations of historical sexual assault by the same individual say they want to tell their stories publicly despite several publication bans in the case. They hope to use a 2023 amendment to the Criminal Code that makes it easier for complainants to waive a publication ban on their names and anything that would identify them.

items.title: Trump's controversial ballroom is getting a new push. How did we get here?
items.summary_auto: The Trump administration is ramping up its push for the president's proposed ballroom after last weekend's shooting at the White House correspondents' dinner, with top Republicans now arguing the event space is needed for security reasons.

items.title: How multi-million dollar speculative betting could convert climate change skeptics
items.summary_auto: An investigation over whether temperature sensors at the Paris airport were tampered with to manipulate winning bets online is putting a new focus on the growing popularity of climate-related wagers.

items.title: Lake Huron's 'fish city' is a sign of trouble at nuclear plant, says Ontario First Nation
items.summary_auto: Saugeen Ojibway Nation is challenging the portrayal of footage of a 'fish city' in the warm waters near Bruce Nuclear Generating Station as a 'fish trap,' raising questions about ecological costs as the operator seeks to raise reactor limits.

items.title: Southern Ontario drug traffickers prompt state of emergency in Long Lake #58 First Nation
items.summary_auto: As a state of emergency remains in place in Long Lake #58 First Nation over the toxic drug crisis, the northwestern Ontario community is creating new safety and wellness roles to help protect vulnerable members.

items.title: Report into Bondi Beach mass shooting calls for increased security at Jewish public events, more gun reforms
items.summary_auto: An interim report into last year's Bondi Beach mass shooting advises increased security around Jewish public events and further gun reforms among 14 initial recommendations, but found Australia's legal and regulatory frameworks did ‌not hinder security agencies in preventing or responding to the attack.

items.title: Powell says he'll remain on U.S. Federal Reserve board, denying Trump a chance to name his own appointee
items.summary_auto: Jerome Powell said Wednesday he plans to remain on the board of the Federal Reserve after his term as chair ends next month "for a period of time, to be determined," saying the "unprecedented" legal attacks by the Trump administration have put the independence of the nation's central bank at risk.

items.title: Canada selected to headquarter new multinational defence bank, sources say
items.summary_auto: Canada has been selected to headquarter the multinational Defence, Security and Resilience Bank (DSRB), sources have told Radio-Canada.

items.title: Changes to disability tax credit broadly welcomed by opposition, advocates
items.summary_auto: The Liberal government's plan to make it easier for Canadians living with disabilities to apply for the disability tax credit is being welcomed by the opposition and advocates who say the proposed changes are long overdue, though some say they don't go far enough.

items.title: Prosecution alleges singer D4vd killed 14-year-old girl to silence her
items.summary_auto: Prosecutors said on Wednesday that singer D4vd killed 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez by stabbing her multiple times.
